segments.s355-g73: ز is the poetic short form of از (here marking position/relation: 'before/at' or 'in the presence of' the Caesar). چو means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بنشید is the past 3rd-person singular of بنشستن ('to sit down'); in classical Persian the verb can precede its subject. فرخ زریر is a personal name (divided into two words here) and functions as the subject of بنشید rather than an adjective.
segments.s355-g74: غمی شد is an idiomatic copular construction: غم + شدن = 'sorrow came' / 'became sorrowful', where شد is the past of شدن ('to become'). ز before پاسخ gives the causal/ablative sense 'because of / from the reply'. فروماند here is an intransitive past meaning 'remained / was left / failed to arrive' (i.e., 'was delayed'). دیر is an adverbial/temporal qualifier 'long (of time)' or 'late', modifying فروماند. The implied subject ('he') is carried over from the previous clause (Farrokh-Zarrir).
segments.s355-g33: ازان (from those/of those) + ویژگان (the distinguished) forms a genitive/identifying phrase 'of those distinguished ones'. پنج تن را is the direct object ('five persons') marked by را for definiteness/emphasis. ببرد is a past action verb here ('he took/carried off'); in classical Ferdowsi Persian the prefix be- appears with the past stem to form the 3rd-person past.
segments.s355-g34: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that') and بودند is the plural past of 'to be' ('they were'). با here means 'with' and links to a series of nouns/adjectives (مغز و هشیار و گرد) that describe those five; the adjectives are coordinated by و. In context مغز is used figuratively for mental ability ('clever'), هشیار 'alert/aware', and گرد functions as another quality (here 'active/going about').
segments.s355-g35: چو here means 'when'. نزدیک درگاه قیصر is a prepositional/locative phrase 'near Caesar's court' (نزدیک 'near' + درگاه 'court' + قیصر 'Caesar'). رسید is the past tense verb 'arrived/reached', so the clause reads 'when (he) came near Caesar's court'.
segments.s355-g36: به here marks direction/place ('to/at'); درگاه سالار بارش is a noun phrase meaning 'the court (درگاه) [of] the commander (سالار) of the baggage/load (بار) — ش is the enclitic possessive 'his' attached to بار'. بدید is the past tense 'he saw'. Word order places the location first ('at the court') followed by the person seen.
segments.s355-g37: 'به در' is a locative prepositional phrase meaning 'at/to the gate' (preposition + noun). 'بر' here functions as a preposition 'on/upon' and takes the following noun phrase 'همه فرش دیبا' ('all the brocade carpets') as its object — word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect. 'کشید' is the past tense verb ('he spread/laid out'), concluding the clause; in epic Persian the verb often follows several prepositional and nominal phrases.
segments.s355-g38: 'بیامد' is a simple past motion verb 'he came.' 'به قیصر' is the destination phrase 'to the Caesar.' 'بگفت' is the past form 'he said / he told' (the prefix b- is a classical verbal prefix). The embedded clause 'آنچ دید' ('that which he saw') is the direct object of 'بگفت' and literally means 'what he had seen,' so the whole clause means 'he came to the Caesar and told him what he had seen.'
segments.s355-g39: 'به کاخ اندرون' is a locative phrase 'in/at the inner palace' (preposition + noun + 'inner/within'). 'بود' is the past copula 'was.' The subject 'قیصر' follows the verb (inverted order common in poetry), and 'دژم' is a predicative adjective 'sullen/grim' describing the Caesar.
segments.s355-g40: 'چو' here is temporal 'when.' The following coordination 'قالوس و گشتاسپ' lists two persons; 'با او' means 'with him' (i.e., accompanying the Caesar), and 'بهم' is an adverb 'together.' The clause is elliptical (no new finite verb) and ties back to the previous clause, implying that these persons were together with him at that time.
segments.s355-g49: بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him/unto him'. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (classical Persian often places the verb before its subject). قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar/emperor'. فرخ‌زاد را is the definite/direct object marked by را, so the phrase means 'the Caesar said to him (concerning) Farrokhzad.'
segments.s355-g50: نپرسی is a negative present/subjunctive or prohibitive form of پرسیدن ('do not ask' / 'you did not ask' in older usage); here it functions as an admonition 'do not ask'. نداری is predicative 'you do not have' / 'you lack' (literally 'there is no having for you'). به دل is a locative/idiomatic phrase 'in the heart', and داد را (داد + object marker را) means 'the giving' or 'it to give' (داد here as 'gave/giving' treated as the thing referred to). Together the clause says roughly 'do not ask; you lack in your heart the giving (i.e. you do not have the heart to give it).'
segments.s355-g51: به قیصر is the dative phrase 'to the Caesar'. چنین is an adverb 'thus' or 'in this way'. گفت is the past tense 'said'. فرخ زریر is the speaker's name; in classical Persian the subject can follow the verb, so the sequence reads 'thus said Farakh Zarrir to the Caesar.'
segments.s355-g52: که introduces reported speech here ('that'). این بنده = 'this servant' (بنده used as 'servant' or humble self-reference). از بندگی = 'of/from servitude' (از + noun indicating cause or relation). گشت is the past of گشتن meaning 'became' (i.e. came to be). سیر is 'sated/weary' (i.e. tired of something). Together: 'that this servant has become weary of servitude.'
segments.s355-g53: گریزان is a participial/adverbial form 'fleeing' or 'on the run' modifying the verb. بیامد is the past 'he came'. ز درگاه شاه = 'from the king's court/presence' (ز = from, درگاه = royal court/presence, شاه = king). The phrase means 'fleeing, he came from the king's court.'
segments.s355-g54: کنون = 'now'. یافت is the past 'found' (he found). ایدر (poetic form of این‌در/اینجا) = 'here'. چنین = 'such'. پایگاه = 'base/stronghold/refuge'. Word order places the verb before its complements, so: 'Now he found here such a stronghold.'
segments.s355-g55: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گشتاسپ is the proper name (the king). بشنید is the past 'he heard'. پاسخ نداد is a negated verbal phrase 'gave no answer' (پاسخ = answer, نداد = did not give). The clause reads 'When Gushtasp heard, he gave no answer.'
segments.s355-g56: تو گفتی = 'you said' (second person past). ز ایران = 'of/from Iran' (here marking the content of the remembrance). نیامدش is 'did not come to him' (the clitic -ش refers back to the thing that might come to him, i.e. یاد). یاد = 'memory/remembrance'. The whole clause means 'you said the remembrance/memory of Iran did not come to him.'
segments.s355-g57: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar / emperor'. شنید is simple past 3rd‑singular 'heard'. این سخن is the direct object 'this speech'. زان (از + آن contracted) here means 'from that' and links the speech to its speaker. جوان is a noun meaning 'young man' and completes the prepositional phrase 'from that young man'. Word order follows older Persian: temporal clause + subject + verb + object + source phrase.
segments.s355-g58: پراندیشه شد is composed of the adjective پراندیشه 'full of thought / pensive' plus شد, the past‑tense/copula 'became' (3rd sg.), so 'became pensive'. مرد روشن‌روان is the noun phrase identifying who became so: مرد 'man' with the epithet روشن‌روان 'bright‑souled'. The predicate (became pensive) appears before the identifying noun phrase—a common poetic inversion.
segments.s355-g59: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that'). شاید is a modal adverb 'perhaps/it may be'. بدن here is poetic/metaphorical — literally 'body' — used to mean the person/agent connected with the speech (i.e. 'the one behind it'); this is a poetic usage. این سخن is 'this speech'; کو bگفت is an archaic relative/relative‑question construction meaning 'who said/uttered it' (کو used here in an older sense as a relative 'who/that', and بگفت is past 'he said').
segments.s355-g60: جز از ... نیست اندر نهفت is a negative existential clause: جز 'except', از راستی 'from truth' (i.e. 'but the truth'), نیست 'is not / there is not', اندر نهفت 'hidden within' (نهفت = hidden/concealed). Together: 'nothing is concealed except (that it is) truth' or more naturally 'it conceals nothing but truth.' Word order places the negation and existential verb before the locative phrase (اندر نهفت).
segments.s355-g61: به قیصر 'to the Caesar' (prepositional object). ز لهراسپ 'from Lohrasp' (source). پیغام 'message' is the direct object and داد is the past verb 'gave / sent' (3rd sg.). Classical Persian permits the order 'to‑X from‑Y object verb'.
segments.s355-g62: که‌گر is the fused conditional 'که‌گر' = 'if'. دادگر is a substantive/adjective 'just, a just one' (here 'a just ruler'). سر نه پیچد is a negative present/subjunctive clause: سر 'head' (used metonymically for 'the person' or 'his conduct'), نه negates the verb, and پیچد is present/subjunctive 'turns / deviates' (the subjunctive here expresses admonition or potential). ز داد 'from justice' completes the phrase: 'do not turn/turn away from justice.' The whole gives a conditional admonition: 'if you are just, do not deviate from justice.'
segments.s355-g63: ازین پس is a temporal phrase 'from now on / henceforth' (از + این + پس). نشستم here functions as a past/perfective verb 'I sat / I am settled' (in older Persian narration this can mean 'I stayed' or 'I resolve to remain'); note that some dictionaries list a different lemma but the context and form indicate the verb 'نشستن' used statively. برومست is an archaic/poetic word (here treated as an ornamental or emphatic predicate 'to be at rest / settled'); و بس 'and enough' is a closing/exclamatory phrase 'and that is enough.' The clause is idiomatic and somewhat compressed in poetic style.
segments.s355-g64: به ایران 'in/at Iran'. نمانیم is 1st‑plural present/subjunctive/optative 'we do not remain' or 'let us not remain'; in context it expresses decision about staying/remaining. بسیار کس literally 'many people' (بسیار = many, کس = person). Together: 'we will not leave many people in Iran' / 'many people will not remain in Iran' — the exact agency is compressed in the poem, but the structure is prepositional phrase + negative verb + noun phrase.
segments.s355-g17: بگفت (past 3sg) is a narrative past 'he said'; این is the demonstrative 'this' (referring to what was just said); و here functions as the narrative connector 'and then'. برساخت is a past 3sg verb 'raised/constructed/assembled' and is the main verb of the clause. در حال کار is an adverbial phrase ('in a state/moment of action' = 'immediately/at once') that modifies برساخت.
segments.s355-g18: گزیده is an adjective 'chosen/selected' modifying the following noun phrase; یکی is an indefinite determiner 'a certain / one' placed before the head noun; لشکری is a nisba/adjectival noun meaning 'army/troops' (here the head noun); نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned/famed'. The whole phrase = 'a chosen, famed army' (one chosen army).
segments.s355-g19: نبیره is a noun 'scion/offspring' (used collectively); برزگان 'the great/notables' and آزادگان 'the free/freemen' are the groups whose scions are meant. The phrase describes the origin/composition: 'scions of the notables and of the free.'
segments.s355-g20: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' marking provenance; کاوس and گودرز are proper names (Kay Kavus and Goudarz). و is the simple conjunction 'and'. کشوادگان here is a rare/archaic term (uncertain in meaning or a family/epithet form) and likely a patronymic or epithet attached to گودرز; its precise sense is uncertain in the lemma list.
segments.s355-g21: ز تخم is a genitival expression 'of the seed/stock (i.e. lineage)'; زرسپ here is a proper name (Zarasp) indicating the ancestor/line. آنک is a relative/particle 'those who/which' or 'that', and بودند is the past plural 'they were'; نیز is the adverb 'also'. Together this yields 'they were also from the line of Zarasp.'
segments.s355-g22: چو is a comparative/illustrative particle 'as / such as' used to introduce examples; بهرام شیراوژن و ریونیز are personal names given as examples of that lineage. The whole segment supplies exemplars: 'such as Bahram Shirāwjan and Rivuniz.'
segments.s355-g23: همی رفت is a habitual/imperfective past construction meaning 'would go / used to go' (here 'rode'); هر is the distributive 'each/every' modifying مهتری 'chief/leader'. با introduces the accompaniment 'with' and اسپ 'steed/horse' is the accompaniment noun (with two steeds is understood from the phrase though 'دو' is not listed in the provided word list). The clause means 'each chief rode with two horses.'
segments.s355-g24: فروزان is an adjective 'radiant/bright' modifying اسپ (placed after the noun as is common); به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/like' (به + کردار = 'in the conduct/style of'), and آذرگشسپ is a proper name (Azar‑Gushasp) serving as the model. So: 'with two radiant steeds, in the manner of Azar‑Gushasp.'
segments.s355-g25: نیاسود is a past negative verb (did not rest/was not at ease) placed before the subject کس in the inverted, poetic word order; تا…به مرز حلب is an extent/adverbial phrase where تا + به + noun means 'as far as/to the border of Aleppo'. Together the segment says that nobody was at peace up to that frontier.
segments.s355-g26: جهان is the grammatical subject and شد is the past of شدن 'became'; the predicate is پر از + noun, where پر 'full' takes از to introduce the contents. The coordinated nouns جنگ و جوش و شغب enumerate what the world became full of (war, a boiling/tumult, and riot).
segments.s355-g27: درفش همایون is a noun phrase (banner + epithet 'auspicious/royal') placed before the verb; برافراختند is past plural 'they raised/erected'. The verb form -ند marks a plural agent (they) and the whole segment narrates the raising of the royal standard.
segments.s355-g28: Two coordinated objects (سراپرده 'inner-chamber' and خیمه‌ها 'tents') appear before the verb ساختند 'they built/erected'; ساختند is past plural matching the implied plural subject, and the coordinating و links both items as what was constructed.
segments.s355-g29: زریر سپهبد is an appositional phrase (name + title). سپه را uses the object marker را to mark 'the army' as the thing affected, and بماند (here a past narrative) means 'was left' / 'he left (it) behind' — an intransitive/old-fashioned use meaning 'left [it] to someone'.
segments.s355-g30: به + proper name marks the recipient/holder ('to Bahram'); گردنکش is an epithet 'the rebellious' attached to Bahram. The conjunction و links the next clause, and خود before براند is emphatic 'he himself' (or 'himself'), with براند a narrative verb 'drove/rode' (he rode off/left).
segments.s355-g31: بسان 'like' introduces a simile; کسی کو is an archaic construction where کو functions as a relative/archaic marker ('someone who'); پیامی برد is a past clause 'carried/took a message' (poetic inversion places the object before the verb). The whole phrase compares the previous actor to such a messenger.
segments.s355-g32: وگر here works as a corrective/contrastive 'but if / or rather'; نزد + شاهی means 'in the presence of the king' (نزد = before/near), and خرامی ('striding proudly') modifies برد (walked/strided). The clause offers an alternative simile: striding proudly before the king.
segments.s355-g9: من (subject) + این پادشاهی (direct object phrase) + مر او را + دهم (verb). من = “I.” این پادشاهی = “this kingship/kingship here.” مر is an archaic/poetic enclitic whose senses listed do not match the dative/recipient use here (so it functions as a clitic marking relation to the recipient). او را = the indirect/accusative phrase ‘him’ with را marking definiteness/object; دهم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative form of دادن (‘I give/I grant’). Word order is subject — object — (recipient clitic) — verb, typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s355-g10: برین is a fused demonstrative/adverbial phrase (بر + ین) meaning “this upon/this on”; بر سرش = “upon his head” with سر (anatomical head) + -ش (his). بر سپاسی نهم contains the verb نهم (1st-person present/subjunctive of نهادن/گذاشتن ‘I place/I put’) and سپاسی as a noun ‘praise/thank-offering/honor’. The repeated preposition بر marks placement ‘on’ and links the physical placement (on his head) to the giving of honor.
segments.s355-g11: Imperative clause: تو (vocative/subject ‘you’) + ز ایدر برو (‘go from here’) where ز = poetic from, ایدر = here, برو = imperative ‘go’. تا حلب = تا used in the sense ‘as far as / to’ (directional) followed by حلب ‘Aleppo’. کینه‌جوی is an epithet/adjective ‘vengeful, vengeance-seeking’ addressing the person. Word order is emphatic: vocative + source + verb + destination + epithet.
segments.s355-g12: Imperative negative prohibition built around مگوی (‘do not say/tell’). سپه را = ‘the army’ + object marker را; جز از جنگ = ‘except from/about war’ (جز ... از = except concerning); چیزی = ‘anything’ used in negative contexts as ‘anything’ (i.e. do not say anything). The clause literally: ‘Do not tell the army anything except about war.’
segments.s355-g13: A simple reporting clause: زریر (proper name) + ستوده (epithet ‘praised’) + به لهراسپ گفت (‘said to Lohrasp’). به is the preposition ‘to’ marking the indirect object; گفت is the past-tense verb ‘said’. Note: the provided sense entries for گفت are noun senses (conversation/discussion) and do not fit the verbal use here, so the sense index is unspecified.
segments.s355-g14: Subordinate clause introduced by که (‘that’). این راز = ‘this secret’; بیرون کشیم is 1st-person plural subjunctive/optative of کشیدن or نهادن-like verbs here meaning ‘let us/let us bring out / we should draw out’ — it expresses the intention/plan after گفت. از نهفت = ‘out of hiding/from concealment’ (نهفت = hidden). The verb form کشیم in the listed senses doesn't match its verbal use here, so the sense index for that lemma is left unspecified.
segments.s355-g15: Conditional clause: گر = ‘if’. اویست is a fused form (اویس + -ست) functioning as a nominal + copula ‘he is Owais’ or ‘he is such’ in context; here it serves to identify the person being described. فرمان‌بر = ‘obedient, subject to command’; و = ‘and’; مهترست = adjective/noun مهتر + copula -ست meaning ‘is chief/greater’. The clause reads ‘If he is obedient and is a chief/elder.’
segments.s355-g16: ورا = dative ‘to/for him’. هرک = ‘whoever’ (indefinite relative). مهتر بود = ‘was greater’ (بود = past of ‘to be’). کهترست = final predicate ‘is lower/inferior’ with present copula -ست; the line thus asserts a general/gnomic result: ‘whoever was greater than him is in fact lesser.’ Note the tense shift (بود past in subordinate clause, -ست present in main predicate) to state a general truth about rank.
segments.s355-g1: The clause has a poetic (verb–subject) order: بنشست is the past verb 'sat down' and لهراسپ (the subject) follows it. پر اندیشه is an adjectival/adverbial phrase 'full of thought' placed before the verb to qualify the action, and دیر at the end is an adverb 'for a long time' modifying the whole clause.
segments.s355-g2: بفرمود is an honorific/past reporting verb 'he ordered/said'. تا introduces a subordinate/result clause here ('so that' / 'and then') and precedes the clause پیش او شد زریر, where شد expresses occurrence/arrival ('came/occurred') and the proper name زریر appears after the verb by poetic inversion.
segments.s355-g3: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' with the dative prepositional pronoun (بدو) before گفت. کاین introduces reported content ('that/this'), and the predicate جز برادرت نیست uses جز ... نیست ('is nothing except') with برادرت (برادر + second-person suffix ت) forming 'your brother'. The negation نیست follows the predicative phrase, typical word order in classical Persian.
segments.s355-g4: بدین چاره functions as 'with/in this remedy/plan' (بدین = 'in/with this' + چاره 'remedy/means'). بشتاب is an imperative 'hasten'. وایدر is an obscure/poetic connective here; مه‌ایست is a verblike equative 'it is the moon' (literally 'the moon it-is'), used here as a temporal or descriptive remark. The whole clause is an exhortation followed by a terse explanatory/temporal note.
segments.s355-g5: درنگ آوری is a conditional/protasis with the verb آوری (here 'bring about' / 'cause' used to mean 'if you cause a delay' or idiomatically 'if you delay'). کار گردد تباه is the apodosis: گردد is the subjunctive/optative 'will become' (or 'become'), and تباه is the predicative adjective 'ruined' describing کار ('the affair'). Word order places the result predicate after the verb, typical of classical style.
segments.s355-g6: This is a direct admonition with two vocatives/objects joined by و: میاسا (a proper name) and اسپ 'steed/horse'. درنگی is the object 'a pause/hesitation' and مخواه is the imperative negative 'do not desire / do not ask for' (i.e., 'do not pause'). The order places vocatives before the prohibition for forceful address.
segments.s355-g7: ببر is the imperative 'take!' placed before its coordinated direct objects تخت 'throne', بالا 'high seat/eminence', and زرینه کفش 'golden shoes' (زرینه modifies کفش). The conjunction و links the nouns in a simple cumulative list.
segments.s355-g8: همان تاج is a demonstrative/identifying phrase 'that same crown' or 'the very crown', and با کاویانی درفش is a prepositional phrase 'with the Kayanian banner/standard' (با = 'with', کاویانی = 'Kavian/Kayanian' as adjective, درفش = 'banner'). The prepositional phrase qualifies which crown is meant.
segments.s355-g41: بدو is a prepositional/indirect-object phrase 'to him' placed before the verb (classical poetic order). آگهی is the direct object 'news/information'. داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' (3rd person singular). سالار بار is a noun phrase 'the commander (سالار) of the baggage/load (بار)' placed after the verb as the sentence's subject (poetic word order allows subject after verb).
segments.s355-g42: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'that it was the case that'). آمد is the simple past 'came'. به is the preposition 'to/into'. درگه is the noun 'royal court'. زریر is the proper name (the subject of the clause), and سوار is an appositive/title 'rider/horseman' attached to the name. Word order here is verb + phrase + name (typical of Ferdowsi's poetic flexibility).
segments.s355-g43: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar'. شنید is the past tense 'heard' (3rd person). این سخن is the direct object 'these words/this speech'. بار داد here is a noun + verb sequence: بار ('occasion; on that occasion') with داد 'gave' — in context a terse idiomatic phrase indicating an action/response given upon hearing (poetic compression); word order places the verbal nucleus after the object phrase.
segments.s355-g44: ازان is a compound prepositional/adverbial 'from that / thereupon' referring back to the reported arrival. آمدن is the verbal noun 'the coming/arrival'. گشت is the past of گشتن here with the sense 'became' (linking verb). گشتاسپ is the subject (proper name), and شاد is the predicative adjective 'glad/happy'. The clause literally reads 'Thereupon (ازان) the coming (آمدن) became Gushtasp glad' — poetically inverted: 'Gushtasp became glad because of that coming.'
segments.s355-g45: زریر is the subject/name. اندر آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'entered/ came in' with اندر as a locative/adverb 'in/within' placed between name and verb (poetic ordering). چو here functions as a simile 'like/as'. سرو بلند is the simile phrase 'a tall cypress' (سرو 'cypress' used metaphorically for a tall, graceful man, modified by بلند 'tall').
segments.s355-g46: نشست is the past 'sat' (3rd person). از بر تخت is a classical/poetic prepositional expression meaning 'upon/on the throne' (از بر as a fused locative in epic Persian). آن is the demonstrative 'that/the', and ارجمند is an adjective 'esteemed/noble' modifying تخت. Word order places verb before its locative complement and modifier after the noun.
segments.s355-g47: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' marking source ('asked of'). قیصر is the indirect object 'Caesar' (the one asked). بپرسید is the past-tense verb 'he asked/inquired' (3rd person). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. پوزش is the noun 'pardon/apology', and گرفت is the past tense 'took/obtained' — together پوزش گرفت means 'he obtained/received pardon'.
segments.s355-g48: همان is the demonstrative 'the same/that'. رومیان is the noun 'the Romans' (here the Byzantine/Roman people); را is the definite object marker marking رومیان as an object/topic. فروزش is a noun 'radiance/splendor' and گرفت is the past 'took/seized'. Grammatically this short clause places the object phrase (همان رومیان را) before the verb phrase (فروزش گرفت); the lineically compressed meaning is that he (or his action) took/seized the Romans' splendor—an idiomatic, poetic way to say he overcame or stripped them of their glory.
segments.s355-g65: This is a sequence of imperatives: تو (vocative 'you') + ز ایدر ('from here') gives the departure point; برو is the imperative 'go'; گو is another imperative 'say' used in coordination (go-and-say); بیارای here is an imperative/command meaning 'array/prepare' (poetic) taking جنگ ('war') as its object. Word order is 'you — from here — go — say — prepare (the) war.' The object جنگ is the thing to be prepared.
segments.s355-g66: سخن ('the word/message') begins a subordinate temporal clause: چون شنیدی = 'when you hear' (چون here = 'when/since'); شنیدی is 2nd-person past/perfect 'you heard/you are told' (archaic -دی ending). نباید is the impersonal/modal 'one must not / you must not,' followed by the bare verb درنگ ('delay') — so 'when you hear the word of war, you must not delay.'
segments.s355-g67: نه starts a negation ('not'/'no'); ایران خزر گشت is a compact clause 'Iran turned/become Khazar' with گشت = 'became/turned.' The conjunction و links on to الیاس من, a vocative/possessive phrase 'my Elias' (poetic order: name + من). The line negates or contrasts the claim about Iran and then addresses Elias.
segments.s355-g68: که introduces a relative clause describing الیاس: 'who/that.' سر برکشیدی is past tense 'you/ he lifted up (your/his) head' (برکشیدی = raised up); از آن انجمن = 'from that assembly' (از = from, آن = that, انجمن = assembly). The clause is linked to الیاس as its modifier (who raised his head from that gathering).
segments.s355-g69: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'thus gave answer' (چنین = 'thus/so'; داد = past 'gave'; پاسخ = 'answer'). که introduces reported speech or a content clause: من جنگ را = 'I (will) [the] war (object marked by را)' — the را marks جنگ as definite/accusative and ties it into the verb that follows in the next segment.
segments.s355-g70: بیازم is a poetic present/subjunctive/volitive form meaning 'I will open/let loose/break forth' (here expressing intention to commence action); همی is an adverb 'always / ever' placed before the rest of the predicate; هر سوی = 'every direction/toward every side'; چنگ را is the object 'the harp' (here metaphorical for force/instrument) with را as the definite object marker. Word order places the verb before its directional and object complements (verb — adverb — directional phrase — object).
segments.s355-g71: تو اکنون frames the addressee: 'you, now.' فرستاده‌ای is a perfect/participial predicate 'you are sent / you have been sent' (poetic/state meaning 'you are the envoy'); بازگرد is an imperative 'return' (go back). The structure states the addressee's role and commands them to go back.
segments.s355-g72: بسازیم is 1st-person plural subjunctive/optative 'let us/may we make' or a future-oriented 'we will make/prepare' (here 'we will prepare'); ناچار is an adverb meaning 'necessarily/of necessity' qualifying the action; جای نبرد is a noun phrase 'place of battle' (جای = place, نبرد = battle). The verb precedes its object (بسازیم ... جای نبرد) in poetic word order.

sentences.s355-s37: When Farrokh-Zarrir sat before the Caesar, sorrow came upon him — the reply was long delayed.
sentences.s355-s17: He took five of those distinguished men, who were intelligent, alert, and active.
sentences.s355-s18: When he drew near Caesar's court, he saw at the court the commander of the baggage.
sentences.s355-s19: At the gate he spread brocade carpets everywhere; he came to the Caesar and told him what he had seen.
sentences.s355-s20: In the inner palace the Caesar sat sullen; when Qalus and Gushtasp were with him together.
sentences.s355-s25: The Caesar said to him, “Do not ask about Farrokhzad; you have not the heart to give it.”
sentences.s355-s26: Farakh Zarrir said thus to the Caesar: 'This servant has grown weary of servitude.'
sentences.s355-s27: Fleeing, he came from the king's court; now here he found such a refuge.
sentences.s355-s28: When Gushtasp heard, he made no reply; you said the memory of Iran did not come to him.
sentences.s355-s29: When the Caesar heard this speech from that young man, the bright‑souled man became thoughtful.
sentences.s355-s30: for perhaps the very person who uttered this speech concealed nothing but the truth.
sentences.s355-s31: Lohrasp sent a message to the Caesar: if a ruler is just, let him not turn his head away from justice.
sentences.s355-s32: From now on I sit (am settled) — enough — and we will not leave many people in Iran.
sentences.s355-s9: He said this and immediately mustered a chosen, renowned army.
sentences.s355-s10: Made up of scions of the nobles and of the freemen, from Kay Kavus and Goudarz Keshvadagan.
sentences.s355-s11: They were also of the seed of Zarasp — for example Bahram Shirāwjan and Rivuniz.
sentences.s355-s12: Each chief rode with two shining steeds, in the manner of Azar‑Gushasp.
sentences.s355-s13: No one found rest as far as the border of Aleppo; the world became full of war, turmoil, and riot.
sentences.s355-s14: They raised the auspicious (royal) standard and set up the inner chambers and tents.
sentences.s355-s15: Zarir, the spahbed, left the army to Bahram the rebellious, and he himself rode away.
sentences.s355-s16: Like someone who carried a message — or else, like one who strides proudly before a king.
sentences.s355-s5: I give him this kingship; upon his head I set this and grant him honor.
sentences.s355-s6: Go from here to Aleppo, O vengeance-seeker; tell the army nothing except matters of war.
sentences.s355-s7: Zarir the Praised said to Lohrasp that we should bring this secret out of hiding.
sentences.s355-s8: If he is obedient and chief, then whoever was greater than him is in fact inferior.
sentences.s355-s1: Lohrasp sat down, full of thought, for a long time; he commanded, and Zarihr came before him.
sentences.s355-s2: He said to him, 'This is none other than your brother; hasten with this remedy — do not delay; the moon is (now) present.'
sentences.s355-s3: If you delay, the affair will be ruined; Miāsa and steed, seek no pause.
sentences.s355-s4: Take the throne and the high seat and the golden shoes — that crown with the Kayanian standard.
sentences.s355-s21: The commander of the baggage gave him the news that Zareer the horseman had come to the court.
sentences.s355-s22: When the Caesar heard these words he acted/gave a response; from that coming Gushtasp became glad.
sentences.s355-s23: Zareer entered; tall as a cypress, he sat upon that noble throne.
sentences.s355-s24: He asked of the Caesar and obtained pardon; he took from those same Romans their splendor.
sentences.s355-s33: You, go from here and say: 'Make ready for war.' When you hear word of battle, you must not delay.
sentences.s355-s34: Not that Iran turned Khazar, nor (my) Elias — who raised his head from that assembly.
sentences.s355-s35: Thus he answered: 'I will unleash the war; I will ever send forth my harp (i.e. my force) to every direction.'
sentences.s355-s36: You are now the envoy; return. We will of necessity make ready a place for battle.
